reason: The statement applies because the text fragment mentions Samuel speaking on behalf of the LORD regarding the anointing of Saul as king over his people, Israel, and the consequences of Saul's disobedience to the LORD's commandments. While the text does not explicitly mention a Messiah or Messiah-like figure in this specific context, the concept of an anointed one (Saul in this case) chosen by Yahweh to lead and save the Hebrews aligns with the broader idea of a chosen deliverer or savior, thus linking to the concept of a Messiah in a broader sense.
